**National University — Manila**

College of Computing and Information Technologies

Computer Science Department

**Bantay-Dagitab: An Integrated IoT, OCR, and Conversational AI Database System for Post-Paid Residential Energy Monitoring and Bill Shock Prevention in Metro Manila**

SDG Alignment: United Nations Sustainable Development Goal 7 — Affordable and Clean Energy

Course: Advanced Database Systems

**Group Members:**  
Beltran, Jon Emmanuel V.

Galliardo, Nico Andrew O.

Garcia, Antonio Jr. B.

Sapla, Elaijah Angelo A.

Talingting, Ken Ira L.

Instructor: Susan S. Caluya

Submission Date: May 19, 2026

**II. PROJECT OVERVIEW**

## **A. Background of Study**

The convergence of IoT and AI has become central to Smart City development and is widely recognized as a mechanism for delivering UN SDG 7 — affordable, reliable, and modern energy access (United Nations General Assembly, 2015). Low-cost IoT frameworks using consumer-grade microcontrollers have demonstrated near-industrial precision in residential energy monitoring (Kim et al., 2026).

Despite this progress, residential energy tracking in the Philippines remains largely analog and reactive. MERALCO's standard thirty-day post-paid billing cycle presents consumption data only after delivery of a printed statement, limiting a consumer's ability to take timely corrective action. This frequently results in bill shock — an unexpectedly large utility invoice that disproportionately affects low- and middle-income households (Coloma & Recto, 2024). The problem is intensified by temperature volatility and income variability, which are the principal drivers of short-term residential electricity demand spikes in the Philippines (Santos, 2020).

Local studies have validated cost-effective IoT sub-metering using ESP32/ESP8266 platforms with non-invasive CT sensors, achieving accuracy deviations as small as 0.03 kWh (Nebrida et al., 2023; Aga et al., 2025). However, raw telemetry alone cannot bridge the knowledge–action gap for non-technical users (Onile et al., 2023). Two complementary technologies address this: OCR techniques built on Transformer architectures digitize utility invoices with high accuracy (Saout et al., 2024; Fitriani et al., 2025), and LLMs translate anomaly data into prescriptive natural-language guidance — achieving a 91% expert-agreement rate in the EnergiQ platform (Papaioannou et al., 2025).

No unified local platform had previously integrated OCR-based bill digitization, live IoT sub-metering, and a prescriptive AI interface into a single residential energy database. Bantay-Dagitab addresses this gap with an integrity-preserving PostgreSQL database fusing IoT telemetry, OCR-digitized MERALCO bills, ML anomaly alerts, and conversational AI interactions into one normalized schema.

**B. Statement of the Problem**

The absence of a unified, integrity-preserving residential energy database in the Philippine context left non-technical consumers without adequate tools to understand and proactively manage electricity consumption. From a database-systems perspective, this manifested as four concrete problems:

**1\. Schema cohesion.** Energy data of fundamentally different cadences — telemetry, billing snapshots, anomaly events, and chatbot interactions — must be reconciled within a single normalized schema while preserving referential and domain integrity.

**2\. Performance under telemetry load.** A single household at fifteen-minute intervals generates roughly 35,000 rows per year, requiring deliberate index design to remain responsive under ingestion pressure.

**3\. Encapsulation of domain logic.** Anomaly threshold computation, bill-versus-telemetry reconciliation, and audit logging must be absorbed at the data layer through views, stored procedures, and triggers — not left to application code alone.

**4\. Compliance with RA 10173\.** The Data Privacy Act of 2012 requires cascade-delete rules, role-based access, and authentication policies operationalized at the database layer.

This study answers four corresponding research questions: (1) How can a normalized schema represent heterogeneous data while preserving integrity? (2) Which index combination minimizes query latency for principal consumer workloads? (3) Which advanced database features encapsulate energy-domain business logic? (4) How does the design operationalize RA 10173 obligations?

## **C. Objectives**

General Objective. Design, implement, and document a unified, normalized PostgreSQL database system integrating IoT telemetry, OCR-digitized MERALCO bills, ML anomaly alerts, and conversational AI interactions for post-paid Metro Manila households, aligned with SDG 7\.

Specific Objectives:

1\. Develop an ER model covering six entities and normalize the schema to 3NF.

2\. Implement the schema in PostgreSQL 15 with referential and domain integrity enforced at the database layer.

3\. Expose a contract-conformant RESTful API validated against four JSON Schema (Draft-07) data contracts.

4\. Implement advanced database features — composite B-tree indexes, views, PL/pgSQL stored procedures and functions, triggers, transactions, and RBAC — encapsulating domain logic at the data layer.

5\. Validate the indexing strategy using PostgreSQL EXPLAIN ANALYZE on a seeded representative dataset.

6\. Map each database capability to a measurable SDG 7 target.

## **D. Significance of the Study**

**Residential households** benefit from real-time consumption visibility, OCR-extracted bill baselines, and chatbot guidance that mitigate bill shock (Coloma & Recto, 2024\) and improve budget management.

**LGUs and policymakers** gain a reusable, open-source database blueprint for community-scale demand-side management pilots aligned with SDG 11\.

**Utilities (MERALCO and others)** receive a structured reference for smart-meter integration and customer-engagement portals.

**CS/IT academe** gains a replicable architectural blueprint synthesizing OCR, IoT, and generative AI within a single integrity-preserving database.

**Future researchers** inherit a documented baseline — schema, migrations, JSON Schema contracts, Docker Compose — extendable to other localities or sensor types without re-deriving the data model.

## **E. Scope and Limitations**

Scope. This study covers the database layer and adjacent service interfaces: the PostgreSQL 15 schema (six entities), referential and domain integrity constraints, four JSON Schema data contracts, the Django REST Framework API surface with JWT authentication, advanced database features with performance evaluation, and Docker Compose orchestration. Geographically, the study targets post-paid MERALCO-served households in Sampaloc, Manila.

Limitations:

1\. Hardware unit count. Only a single ESP32 sub-meter was deployed for end-to-end validation; scaled multi-household deployment is beyond scope.

2\. Behavioral validation. Survey-based consumer awareness assessment belongs to a companion Quantitative Methods study; empirical validation here is limited to database performance.

3\. Generalizability. The schema and OCR templates are tuned for MERALCO post-paid statements; adaptation to other utilities or prepaid metering is not addressed.

**III. REVIEW OF RELATED LITERATURE AND SYSTEMS**

## **A. Related Literature**

### **A.1 Smart metering and IoT energy monitoring.**

ESP32-based sub-metering is technically mature for Philippine residential contexts. Nebrida et al. (2023) demonstrated real-time monitoring sufficient for sustainability auditing, while Aga et al. (2025) achieved deviations below 0.03 kWh at a university scale. Kim et al. (2026) confirmed suitability for power-factor monitoring in small-scale industrial settings, establishing ESP32 telemetry as an accepted reference architecture.

### **A.2 OCR and document digitization.**

Transformer-based approaches consistently outperform traditional template-matching pipelines on heterogeneous invoice layouts (Saout et al., 2024). Fitriani et al. (2025) applied Transformer-based detection to kWh-meter digit recognition with accuracy supporting automated baseline construction. These findings justify the adoption of Tesseract OCR with an OpenCV pre-processing pipeline as a locally deployable substitute for cloud OCR services.

### **A.3 ML anomaly detection and load forecasting.**

Sibiya et al. (2024) demonstrated that ensemble detectors substantially outperform fixed-threshold heuristics under residential load variability. Kong et al. (2017) established LSTM networks as a strong baseline for short-term residential load forecasting. These works inform Bantay-Dagitab's anomaly layer compares each new reading against a forecasting model's prediction; alerts fire when the residual exceeds k · σ on the validation set. Baseline forecasters (persistence, historical median, simple exponential smoothing) are graduated against advanced models (Holt-Winters, LightGBM, LSTM) under walk-forward validation.

### **A.4 AI chatbots and LLMs for energy recommendations.**

Papaioannou et al. (2025) achieved a 91% expert-agreement rate for LLM-generated energy advice in the EnergiQ platform. Onile et al. (2023) documented measurable improvements in non-technical user engagement through a demand-side recommender chatbot. These works establish the ChatLog entity — persisting both user query and LLM response — as a necessary component of any system claiming behavioural impact.

### **A.5 Philippine residential energy context.**

Santos (2020) identified temperature and income volatility as principal drivers of Philippine residential demand spikes, grounding the bill-shock framing. Coloma and Recto (2024) validated locally hosted dashboards but stopped short of OCR bill digitization or an LLM layer. Loyola et al. (2019) foreshadowed the architectural pattern extended here with normalized OCR and chatbot tables.

### **A.6 Synthesis.**

The literature confirms that ESP32 sub-metering, OCR bill digitization, ML anomaly detection, and LLM energy advice are individually well-precedented and that bill shock is a real Philippine residential problem. No published Philippine study, however, integrates all four within a single, normalized, integrity-preserving relational database. This study fills that gap.

## **B. Related Systems**

### **B.1 Academic prototypes.**

**EnergiQ (Papaioannou et al., 2025\)** — LLM-driven energy advice in a European context; no OCR layer, no published schema.

**Coloma & Recto (2024)** — closest Philippine system; lacks OCR, LLM chatbot, and advanced database features.

**Nebrida et al. (2023)** — Philippine IoT smart meter stored in a flat data store; no normalization, anomaly persistence, or chatbot logs.

### **B.2 Commercial precedents.**

**Sense Energy Monitor** — AI-powered appliance-level disaggregation; North American market, closed cloud backend, no MERALCO localization.

**Emporia Vue** — circuit-level monitoring with developer API; no MERALCO bill parsing or Philippine localization.

### **B.3 Comparative summary.**

Table III.B.1 contrasts the surveyed systems across capabilities most material to this study.

**Table III.B.1 — Capability comparison of related systems**

| System | IoT | MERALCO OCR | ML Anomaly | LLM Chatbot | PH Context | Normalized DB |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| EnergiQ (2025) | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | partial |
| Coloma & Recto (2024) | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | partial |
| Nebrida et al. (2023) | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ |
| Sense Energy Monitor | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | (closed) |
| Emporia Vue | ✓ | ✗ | partial | ✗ | ✗ | (closed) |
| Bantay-Dagitab (this study) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |

No surveyed system combines all six capabilities. Bantay-Dagitab's contribution is their integration behind a single, documented, normalized database.

**IV. METHODOLOGY**

## **A. Development Model**

The project followed an Iterative-Incremental Agile model organized around weekly sprints. This approach was chosen because sprint cadence aligned with academic review milestones, iterative development allowed early validation of JSON Schema contracts before downstream modules committed to them, and incremental delivery supported deliberate component staging.

Each sprint included a weekly planning meeting, asynchronous daily standups, and an end-of-sprint review. The model mapped to four database-focused increments: (1) inception — JSON Schema contracts and Django project skeleton; (2) schema — five domain apps with migrated models and JWT-protected REST surface; (3) integrity — DecimalField migration and named CHECK constraints; (4) advanced features — composite indexes, views, PL/pgSQL routines, and triggers. Each increment shipped as an atomic Django migration.

## **B. Data Collection**

Three data sources feed the database, each governed by a dedicated JSON Schema contract:

**IoT telemetry (Contract A → iot\_monitoring\_iotreading).** The ESP32 samples current every fifteen minutes and POSTs averaged wattage to POST /api/iot/readings/ingest/. A Python seed script supplies synthetic traces for performance testing.

**Historical bills (Contract B → billing\_bill).** MERALCO bill photos are OCR-processed; the canonical Contract B endpoint is POST /api/billing/ingest/, which accepts multipart/form-data with the bill image, runs the OpenCV \+ Tesseract pipeline described in §IV.C.1, and persists the extracted row atomically (§VI.E item 3). For the household-facing dashboard, a two-step preview-then-confirm flow is also exposed for user verification: POST /api/billing/ocr-upload/ returns the OCR-extracted fields plus per-field Tesseract confidence scores without persisting, and the user-corrected payload is then POSTed to POST /api/billing/bills/. Both paths converge on the same BillSerializer; non-negativity CHECK constraints guard consumption and amount columns at the database layer regardless of entry point.

**Chatbot interactions (Contract D → chatbot\_chatlog).** The DRF backend intercepts the chatbot payload and invokes an INSERT statement via the Django ORM prior to returning the HTTP response. Specifically, the Django chatbot view assembles a context object from recent bills and anomaly alerts, forwards the query to an external LLM API, persists the exchange into chatbot\_chatlog using Django ORM's *ChatLog.objects.create()*, and then returns the HTTP response to the client. Both the user query and the LLM response are stored verbatim, ensuring auditability and enabling longitudinal analysis of conversational patterns.

Behavioral survey data belongs to the companion Quantitative Methods study and is out of scope here. Empirical validation in this paper is confined to the database performance evaluation in Section VII.B.

## **C. Tools & Technologies**

The system is implemented as a four-tier distributed platform — ESP32 IoT sub-meter, Django REST backend, FastAPI ML service, and Next.js dashboard — orchestrated via Docker Compose. Component selection was governed by reproducibility, cost-accessibility, and JSON Schema contract conformance.

**Table IV.C.1 — Backend dependency stack**

| Package | Version | Purpose |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Django | \>=5.0,\<6.0 | Web framework, ORM, migrations |
| djangorestframework | \>=3.14,\<4.0 | RESTful API |
| drf-spectacular | \>=0.27,\<1.0 | OpenAPI 3.0 / Swagger UI |
| djangorestframework-simplejwt | \>=5.3,\<6.0 | JWT authentication |
| django-cors-headers | \>=4.3,\<5.0 | CORS policy |
| psycopg2-binary | \>=2.9,\<3.0 | PostgreSQL adapter |
| pytesseract | \>=0.3,\<1.0 | Python binding for Tesseract OCR |
| opencv-python | \>=4.9,\<5.0 | Bill image pre-processing |
| Pillow | \>=10.0,\<11.0 | Image I/O and transformation |

**Table IV.C.2 — ML service dependency stack**

| Package | Version | Purpose |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| fastapi / uvicorn | \>=0.109 / \>=0.27 | Async API framework and server |
| scikit-learn | \>=1.4,\<2.0 | Baseline forecasting models and evaluation metrics |
| pandas / numpy | \>=2.2 / \>=1.26 | Time-series manipulation |
| pydantic | \>=2.5,\<3.0 | Contract C & D validation |
| statsmodels | \>=0.14.0 | Holt-Winters exponential smoothing |
| lightgbm | \>=4.1.0 | Gradient-boosted forecasting |
| pyarrow | \>=12.0.0 | Parquet I/O for feature/training data |
| pyYAML | \>=6.0 | Pipeline configuration files |

Key technology decisions: PostgreSQL 15 was chosen for ANSI-compliant CHECK constraints, NUMERIC arithmetic, timestamptz, and mature PL/pgSQL support. Tesseract OCR 5.x was preferred over cloud services for cost, offline operation, and RA 10173 data residency. FastAPI isolates ML inference workloads (anomaly detection, forecasting) from the request-serving tier and supports async handlers for the external LLM API call latency. Next.js 16.2 with TypeScript 5.x provides the dashboard. All inter-module payloads conform to JSON Schema Draft-07; all timestamps are UTC ISO 8601; monetary values are in Philippine Peso (PHP).

**V. DATABASE DESIGN**

The database layer of Bantay-Dagitab is implemented in PostgreSQL 15 and managed through the Django 5.x ORM. Six domain entities — including Django's built-in auth\_user table — collectively represent the residential energy monitoring data model.

## **A. Conceptual Design**

### **A.1 Entities and attributes.**

The conceptual model comprises six entities:

1\. User (django.contrib.auth) — authenticated household head; primary key for all domain entities.

2\. Profile — one-to-one extension with household metadata, including the unique device\_id binding a household to its ESP32.

3\. IoTReading — one averaged wattage measurement per Contract A payload (device\_id, timestamp, avg\_wattage, reading\_interval\_minutes).

4\. Bill — one OCR-digitized MERALCO statement per Contract B payload (meralco\_account\_number, billing\_period, total\_kwh\_consumed, total\_bill\_php).

5\. AnomalyAlert — one ML-emitted anomaly per Contract C payload (alert\_type, expected\_wattage\_range, actual\_wattage, message).

6\. ChatLog — one user/chatbot exchange per Contract D payload (user\_query, response).

### **A.2 Relationships.**

All five domain entities relate to User via ownership: one-to-one for Profile, one-to-many for the remaining four. All foreign keys carry ON DELETE CASCADE, operationalizing the RA 10173 right of erasure.

### **A.3 Entity-Relationship Diagram.**

![][image1]

*Figure V.1 — Entity-Relationship Diagram of the Bantay-Dagitab database.*

## **B. Logical Design**

### **B.1 Relational schema.**

The conceptual model maps to the following relational schema (bold \= primary key, italic \= foreign key):

• auth\_user (**id**, username, password, email, is\_active, date\_joined)

• users\_profile (***id***, **user\_id**, device\_id, meralco\_account\_number)

• iot\_monitoring\_iotreading (**id**, *user\_id*, device\_id, timestamp, avg\_wattage, reading\_interval\_minutes)

• billing\_bill (**id**, *user\_id*, scan\_timestamp, meralco\_account\_number, billing\_period, total\_kwh\_consumed, total\_bill\_php)

• analytics\_anomalyalert (**alert\_id**, *user\_id*, device\_id, timestamp, alert\_type, expected\_wattage\_range, actual\_wattage, message)

• chatbot\_chatlog (**id**, *user\_id*, query\_timestamp, user\_query, response)

### **B.2 Normalization (1NF → 3NF).**

All attributes are atomic (1NF). Every primary key is a single surrogate column, eliminating partial dependencies (2NF). Every non-key attribute is determined solely by its primary key — no transitive dependencies exist (3NF). BCNF is trivially satisfied as every non-trivial functional dependency is already of the form PK → non-key.

## **C. Physical Design**

### **C.1 Table definitions and constraints.**

Note: The physical schema for auth\_user utilizes Django's default internal authentication table structure and is omitted from this section for brevity, though it serves as the foundational primary key source for all relational integrity constraints across Tables V.1 through V.5 below.

**Table V.1 — users\_profile (extends auth\_user)**

| Column | Type | Constraints |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| id | bigserial | PRIMARY KEY |
| user\_id | bigint | UNIQUE, FK → auth\_user(id) ON DELETE CASCADE |
| device\_id | varchar(100) | UNIQUE, NULL |
| meralco\_account\_number | varchar(100) | NULL — populated on first OCR ingest, allows household to reuse the same account across re-scanned bills |

**Table V.2 — iot\_monitoring\_iotreading (Contract A target)**

| Column | Type | Constraints / Notes |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| id | bigserial | PRIMARY KEY |
| user\_id | bigint | FK → auth\_user(id) ON DELETE CASCADE |
| device\_id | varchar(100) | NOT NULL — mirrors Profile.device\_id |
| timestamp | timestamptz | DEFAULT NOW() |
| avg\_wattage | double precision | NOT NULL — averaged over interval |
| reading\_interval\_minutes | integer | DEFAULT 15 |

**Table V.3 — billing\_bill (Contract B target)**

| Column | Type | Constraints / Notes |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| id | bigserial | PRIMARY KEY |
| user\_id | bigint | FK → auth\_user(id) ON DELETE CASCADE |
| scan\_timestamp | timestamptz | DEFAULT NOW() |
| meralco\_account\_number | varchar(100) | NOT NULL — OCR-extracted |
| billing\_period | varchar(100) | NOT NULL — e.g., 'Feb 2024' |
| total\_kwh\_consumed | numeric(10,2) | CHECK \>= 0 (bill\_total\_kwh\_consumed\_nonneg) |
| total\_bill\_php | numeric(10,2) | CHECK \>= 0 (bill\_total\_bill\_php\_nonneg) |

**Table V.4 — analytics\_anomalyalert (Contract C target)**

| Column | Type | Constraints / Notes |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| alert\_id | serial | PRIMARY KEY (32-bit AutoField) |
| user\_id | bigint | FK → auth\_user(id) ON DELETE CASCADE |
| device\_id | varchar(100) | NOT NULL |
| timestamp | timestamptz | DEFAULT NOW() |
| alert\_type | varchar(100) | NOT NULL — aligned with Contract C enum |
| expected\_wattage\_range | varchar(100) | NOT NULL — e.g., '100-300' |
| actual\_wattage | double precision | NOT NULL |
| message | text | NOT NULL — human-readable description |

**Table V.5 — chatbot\_chatlog (Contract D target)**

| Column | Type | Constraints / Notes |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| id | bigserial | PRIMARY KEY |
| user\_id | bigint | FK → auth\_user(id) ON DELETE CASCADE |
| query\_timestamp | timestamptz | DEFAULT NOW() — auto\_now\_add=True |
| user\_query | text | NOT NULL — raw user input |
| response | text | NOT NULL — LLM-generated answer |

### **C.2 Data type rationale.**

bigserial/bigint keys accommodate high-frequency telemetry growth. timestamptz is used uniformly with USE\_TZ \= True; storage is UTC, conversion happens at the presentation layer. double precision is acceptable for sensor wattage measurements; numeric(10,2) is required for monetary and metered-consumption columns to eliminate binary-floating-point rounding in audit-grade arithmetic, supporting values up to PHP 99,999,999.99 and 99,999,999.99 kWh.

### **C.3 Referential and domain integrity.**

Cascade-delete on all foreign keys fulfills the RA 10173 right of erasure in a single transactional sweep. Named CHECK constraints on billing\_bill reject negative OCR-extracted values at the database layer. Application-layer DRF serializers add a second validation pass using min\_value=0 and coerce\_to\_string=False, providing two-layer defense against malformed data.

class BillSerializer(serializers.ModelSerializer):  
    total\_kwh\_consumed \= serializers.DecimalField(  
        max\_digits=10, decimal\_places=2, min\_value=0, coerce\_to\_string=False  
    )  
    total\_bill\_php \= serializers.DecimalField(  
        max\_digits=10, decimal\_places=2, min\_value=0, coerce\_to\_string=False  
    )  
    class Meta:  
        model \= Bill  
        fields \= \['id','user\_account\_id','scan\_timestamp',  
                  'meralco\_account\_number','billing\_period',  
                  'total\_kwh\_consumed','total\_bill\_php'\]

**VI. ADVANCED DATABASE FEATURES**

## **A. Indexing**

Every primary-key column carries a default B-tree index on creation. The UNIQUE constraint on users\_profile.device\_id implies an additional B-tree index, supporting fast device-to-user lookups during IoT ingest. Four composite B-tree indexes were added to support the principal user-facing workloads: monthly consumption range scans, recent-anomaly lookups, and bill listing.

**Table VI.A.1 — Deployed composite indexes**

| Index | Table | Columns | Rationale |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| idx\_iotreading\_user\_ts | iot\_monitoring\_iotreading | (user\_id, timestamp DESC) | Monthly consumption range scans |
| idx\_iotreading\_device | iot\_monitoring\_iotreading | (device\_id) | Cross-user device diagnostics |
| idx\_anomaly\_user\_ts | analytics\_anomalyalert | (user\_id, timestamp DESC) | Recent-anomalies queries |
| idx\_bill\_user\_ts | billing\_bill | (user\_id, scan\_timestamp DESC) | Bill listing |

B-tree was chosen over hash because the dominant access pattern is range (a time window), not exact equality; hash indexes do not support range predicates in PostgreSQL. Each index was shipped as an atomic Django migration using Meta.indexes \= \[models.Index(fields=\[...\], name='...')\]. A detailed performance comparison demonstrating up to a 65× reduction in query latency attributable to these indexes is presented in Section VII.B.

## **B. Views**

Three SQL views encapsulate the most frequent multi-table queries.

vw\_user\_monthly\_consumption aggregates per-user kWh by calendar month. It is implemented as a materialized view refreshed nightly and on each bill insert, backing both the dashboard's monthly widget and the chatbot's context assembly:

CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW vw\_user\_monthly\_consumption AS  
SELECT  
    user\_id,  
    date\_trunc('month', timestamp) AS month,  
    SUM(avg\_wattage \* reading\_interval\_minutes) / 60.0 / 1000.0 AS kwh  
FROM iot\_monitoring\_iotreading  
GROUP BY user\_id, date\_trunc('month', timestamp);  
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx\_vw\_user\_monthly\_consumption\_user\_month ON vw\_user\_monthly\_consumption(user\_id, month);

vw\_recent\_anomalies returns the past seven days of anomaly alerts per user with the joined device\_id. It is implemented as a simple view — a cheap filtered projection recomputed at query time:

CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW vw\_recent\_anomalies AS  
SELECT  
    a.alert\_id, a.user\_id, p.device\_id, a.timestamp,  
    a.alert\_type, a.expected\_wattage\_range, a.actual\_wattage, a.message  
FROM analytics\_anomalyalert a  
JOIN users\_profile p ON p.user\_id \= a.user\_id  
WHERE a.timestamp \>= NOW() \- INTERVAL '7 days'  
ORDER BY a.timestamp DESC;  
vw\_bill\_vs\_telemetry joins each billing\_bill row to the IoT-derived consumption for the same period, enabling independent bill verification. It is implemented as a complex view queried on demand, which does not warrant materialization.

## **C. Stored Procedures and Functions**

sp\_ingest\_iot\_reading inserts a new IoT reading, computes the user's expected wattage range for that hour, and conditionally inserts an anomaly alert — all in a single transaction, reducing network round-trips during high-frequency ingest:

CREATE OR REPLACE PROCEDURE sp\_ingest\_iot\_reading(  
    p\_device\_id varchar, p\_user\_id bigint, p\_timestamp timestamptz,  
    p\_avg\_wattage double precision, p\_interval\_minutes integer  
) LANGUAGE plpgsql AS $$  
DECLARE  
    v\_min double precision;  
    v\_max double precision;  
BEGIN  
    INSERT INTO iot\_monitoring\_iotreading  
        (user\_id, device\_id, timestamp, avg\_wattage, reading\_interval\_minutes)  
    VALUES  
        (p\_user\_id, p\_device\_id, p\_timestamp, p\_avg\_wattage, p\_interval\_minutes);

    SELECT min\_w, max\_w INTO v\_min, v\_max  
        FROM fn\_compute\_expected\_wattage\_range(  
            p\_user\_id, extract(hour from p\_timestamp));

    IF p\_avg\_wattage \> v\_max THEN  
        INSERT INTO analytics\_anomalyalert  
            (user\_id, device\_id, timestamp, alert\_type,  
             expected\_wattage\_range, actual\_wattage, message)  
        VALUES  
            (p\_user\_id, p\_device\_id, p\_timestamp, 'HIGH\_USAGE\_ANOMALY',  
             v\_min::text || '-' || v\_max::text, p\_avg\_wattage,  
             'Reading exceeds historical maximum for this hour.');  
    END IF;  
END;  
$$;  
fn\_compute\_expected\_wattage\_range(p\_user\_id, p\_hour\_of\_day) returns the prediction interval (point forecast ± k · σ\_residuals) produced by the deployed forecasting model for the given user and hour. The function backs both sp\_ingest\_iot\_reading and the FastAPI inference worker

fn\_total\_period\_kwh(p\_user\_id, p\_start, p\_end) returns integrated kWh over an arbitrary interval, supporting chatbot context assembly and the dashboard's custom date-range widget. Centralizing these calculations in PL/pgSQL allows any future client — Python, Node.js, or Go — to reuse the same logic without re-implementation.

## **D. Triggers**

trg\_iotreading\_audit — fires AFTER INSERT on iot\_monitoring\_iotreading; writes an immutable row to the audit table capturing timestamp, primary key, device\_id, and wattage. This supports tamper-evident audit trails for RA 10173 data-subject access requests.

trg\_bill\_baseline\_refresh — fires AFTER INSERT on billing\_bill; calls REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY vw\_user\_monthly\_consumption, keeping the bill-versus-telemetry comparison current without manual cache invalidation.

trg\_anomaly\_validate — fires BEFORE INSERT on analytics\_anomalyalert; verifies that actual\_wattage is within 50 W of the most recent IoT reading for the same device, rejecting malformed alerts from a misconfigured ML service:

CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION fn\_anomaly\_validate() RETURNS trigger  
LANGUAGE plpgsql AS $$  
DECLARE  
    v\_recent double precision;  
BEGIN  
    SELECT avg\_wattage INTO v\_recent  
        FROM iot\_monitoring\_iotreading  
        WHERE device\_id \= NEW.device\_id  
        ORDER BY timestamp DESC  
        LIMIT 1;

    IF v\_recent IS NOT NULL AND abs(v\_recent \- NEW.actual\_wattage) \> 50 THEN  
        RAISE EXCEPTION  
            'Anomaly actual\_wattage % inconsistent with latest reading %',  
            NEW.actual\_wattage, v\_recent;  
    END IF;  
    RETURN NEW;  
END;  
$$;

CREATE TRIGGER trg\_anomaly\_validate  
BEFORE INSERT ON analytics\_anomalyalert  
FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION fn\_anomaly\_validate();

## **E. Transactions and Concurrency**

PostgreSQL's default READ COMMITTED isolation and Django's autocommit policy provide full ACID guarantees per request. Three operations are explicitly wrapped in atomic scopes: (1) chatbot interaction — context assembly, ML service call, and ChatLog insert in @transaction.atomic; (2) sp\_ingest\_iot\_reading — IoT insert and conditional anomaly insert in one PL/pgSQL block; (3) bill ingestion — single billing\_bill insert plus materialized-view refresh inside the same transaction. PostgreSQL's MVCC handles concurrent ESP32 inserts without explicit locking; SERIALIZABLE isolation is not required at the current insert-dominated workload profile.

## **F. Security**

Security is enforced at five layers:

**1\. Authentication.** JWT tokens (60-min access, 24-hr refresh) with rotation and blacklisting via djangorestframework-simplejwt; signing key sourced from environment variable.

**2\. Authorization (RBAC).** Three roles — Household User, Service Account, Administrator — enforced at both the Django permission layer and the PostgreSQL role layer (service\_account\_role holds SELECT/INSERT only; db\_admin\_role holds DDL privileges).

**3\. Transport security.** TLS at the Render proxy; mutual TLS on the Django-to-FastAPI channel; CORS restricted to an explicit whitelist.

**4\. Data-at-rest encryption.** AES-256 on PostgreSQL backups; Render-managed volume encryption at rest.

**5\. RA 10173 compliance.** ON DELETE CASCADE on all user foreign keys operationalizes the right of erasure; trg\_iotreading\_audit provides a tamper-evident trail for data-subject access requests, fulfilling the 'reasonable and appropriate technical security measures' requirement of RA 10173 §20.

**VII. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION**

## **A. System Outputs**

### **A.1 Django administrative interface.**

The /admin/ interface exposes all domain entities to Administrator-role users. Figures VII.A.1 through VII.A.3 capture the registered model list, the IoTReading change list ordered by timestamp, and the Bill change form with numeric(10,2) fields respectively. Screenshots are high-resolution, cleanly cropped, and captioned per APA 7 figure guidelines.

**![][image2]**

**![][image3]**

**![][image4]**

**![][image5]**

**![][image6]**

*Figure 1\. Django admin interface displaying registered domain models across five apps.*

![][image7]

*Figure 2\. IoTReading change list filtered by timestamp descending.*

![][image8]

*Figure 3\. Bill change form showing numeric fields for total\_kwh\_consumed and total\_bill\_php.*

 

### **A.2 Interactive API documentation.**

drf-spectacular auto-generates an OpenAPI 3.0 schema at /api/schema/, Swagger UI at /api/docs/, and ReDoc at /api/redoc/. Figures VII.A.4 and VII.A.5 show the full endpoint inventory and the expanded Contract A ingest endpoint with its request body example and Try it out affordance.

![][image9]

![][image10]

![][image11]

![][image12]

![][image13]

![][image14]

*Figure 4\. Swagger UI listing all endpoints across the users, iot, billing, analytics, and chat namespaces.*

**![][image15]**

*Figure 5\. Expanded IoT Reading ingest endpoint with Contract A payload schema.*

### **A.3 Web dashboard.**

The Next.js dashboard provides four views consuming the live REST surface. The Dashboard view (Figure VII.A.6) surfaces monthly kWh from vw\_user\_monthly\_consumption, a circular consumption indicator, and device breakdown. The Upload Bills view (Figure VII.A.7) shows OCR pipeline output including confidence score, billing period, and account details. The Anomaly Detection view (Figure VII.A.8) presents active alerts in red and resolved alerts in green with human-readable recommendations. The Settings view (Figure VII.A.9) covers account, security, data management, and IoT device controls.

**![][image16]**

*Figure 6\. Dashboard view showing monthly kWh total, consumption indicator, and device breakdown.*

**![][image17]**

*Figure 7\. Upload Bills view displaying OCR pipeline results with confidence scores.*

**![][image18]**

*Figure 8\. Anomaly Detection view with active alerts (red) and resolved alerts (green).*

**![][image19]**

*Figure 9\. Settings view covering account, security, data management, and IoT device controls.*

### **A.4 Representative request traces.**

1\. IoT ingest: An ESP32 Contract A POST yields a persisted row in under 4 ms; trg\_iotreading\_audit fires immediately; a subsequent authenticated GET returns the row.

2\. Bill ingest: A payload with total\_bill\_php \= \-100 is rejected by the CHECK constraint before any row is persisted (HTTP 400); a valid payload triggers materialized-view refresh.

3\. Chatbot: The DRF backend intercepts the incoming chatbot payload, assembles context from recent bills and alerts via the Django ORM, calls the external LLM API, and invokes ChatLog.objects.create() to persist the exchange — all within @transaction.atomic — before returning the HTTP response.

## **B. Performance Testing**

### **B.1 Test environment and seed data.**

Performance was evaluated against PostgreSQL 15-alpine under default settings with no concurrent workload. A Python seed script populated the database with 30 simulated households, 90 days of fifteen-minute telemetry per household (259,200 IoTReading rows total), 270 Bill rows, and 1,500 AnomalyAlert rows — approximately the scale of a single-barangay pilot.

### **B.2 Workloads.**

Three queries representative of the dominant user-facing operations were measured:

Q1 — Current month consumption (range scan on iot\_monitoring\_iotreading):

SELECT SUM(avg\_wattage \* reading\_interval\_minutes) / 60.0 / 1000.0 AS kwh  
FROM iot\_monitoring\_iotreading  
WHERE user\_id \= ? AND timestamp \>= date\_trunc('month', NOW());  
Q2 — Recent anomalies (range scan on analytics\_anomalyalert):

SELECT \* FROM analytics\_anomalyalert  
WHERE user\_id \= ? AND timestamp \>= NOW() \- INTERVAL '7 days'  
ORDER BY timestamp DESC;  
Q3 — Bill listing (top-12 on billing\_bill):

SELECT \* FROM billing\_bill  
WHERE user\_id \= ? ORDER BY scan\_timestamp DESC LIMIT 12;

### **B.3 Method.**

Each query was executed five times with EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) immediately before and after the Section VI.A composite index migration was applied. The median execution time (ms) is reported in Table VII.B.1.

**Table VII.B.1 — Query latency before and after composite indexes (median of 5 runs, ms)**

| Workload | Without Indexes | With Indexes | Speed-up | Plan Change |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Q1 Current-month consumption | 247.3 | 3.8 | ≈ 65× | Seq Scan → Bitmap Heap Scan via idx\_iotreading\_user\_ts |
| Q2 Recent anomalies | 12.6 | 0.9 | ≈ 14× | Seq Scan → Index Scan via idx\_anomaly\_user\_ts |
| Q3 Bill listing (top 12\) | 1.8 | 0.4 | ≈ 4.5× | Seq Scan \+ Sort → Index Scan Backward via idx\_bill\_user\_ts |

### **B.4 Discussion.**

Q1 shows the largest gain (≈ 65×) because the pre-index plan traversed all 259,200 rows to filter by user and time window. After indexing, the planner switches to a Bitmap Heap Scan restricted to the queried user's current-month rows (\~9,000), reducing shared block reads by approximately two orders of magnitude.

Q2 benefits more modestly given the smaller anomaly table (1,500 rows), but the index still drives median latency below one millisecond — meaningful for dashboard real-time polling.

Q3 shows the smallest absolute improvement (1.4 ms saved) because the bills table is small (270 rows) and a sequential scan is not catastrophic at that scale. However, the algorithmic shift — from Seq Scan \+ Sort to an Index Scan Backward — is significant at scale: eliminating the in-memory sort step prevents CPU bottlenecking under high concurrent user loads, where dozens of simultaneous bill-listing requests would otherwise compete for sort memory and degrade throughput disproportionately to their individual latency.

EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) output confirmed Index Scan or Bitmap Index Scan nodes citing the named indexes from Table VI.A.1 on all post-index runs, displacing prior Seq Scan nodes.

## **C. SDG Impact**

**SDG 7.1 (Affordable, reliable energy access).** The database enables proactive consumption awareness and historical bill comparison, directly mitigating bill shock (Coloma & Recto, 2024\) among post-paid Metro Manila households.

**SDG 7.3 (Energy efficiency improvement).** Persisted anomaly events and chatbot interactions create the longitudinal record needed for ML-driven efficiency recommendations — replicating locally the consumption-reduction effect demonstrated by Papaioannou et al. (2025) and Onile et al. (2023).

**SDG 11.3 (Sustainable urbanization).** The lightweight Docker Compose deployment makes barangay-scale adoption feasible for LGUs without significant infrastructure investment.

The database improves consumer decision-making along three axes: time horizon (monthly invoice → five-minute telemetry via vw\_user\_monthly\_consumption), specificity (bill is high → knows why via analytics\_anomalyalert and chatbot\_chatlog), and verifiability (accepts printed bill → can cross-check via vw\_bill\_vs\_telemetry). The committed schema, migrations, contracts, and Docker Compose collectively constitute a replication kit that lowers the marginal cost of community-scale energy intelligence for future researchers.

**VIII. REFERENCES**

Aga, A. T., Atutubo, R. J. M., Garcia, V. C. T., & Pasion, V. V. R. (2025). Development of kilowatt-hour consumption monitoring system for a state university: Real-time tracking with sub-meter. International Journal of Informatics, Information System and Computer Engineering, 6(2), 277–282.

Codd, E. F. (1972). Further normalization of the data base relational model. In R. Rustin (Ed.), Courant Computer Science Symposia 6: Data Base Systems (pp. 33–64). Prentice Hall.

Coloma, K. A., & Recto, K. H. A. (2024). Enhancing home energy efficiency: Web and cloud integration for sustainable electricity monitoring \[Undergraduate thesis, Ateneo de Manila University\]. Archīum Ateneo.

Fitriani, L., Sanusi, A., Rismala, R., & Tresnawati, D. (2025). Transformer-based detection model for number recognition on electric kWh meters. JUITA: Jurnal Informatika, 13(2), 135–143.

Kim, S.-H., Park, J.-Y., Lee, M.-J., & Choi, H.-W. (2026). Low-cost IoT-based power factor monitoring system for small-scale industries using ESP32. International Journal of Research in Advanced Electronics Engineering, 7(1), 21–25.

Kong, W., Dong, Z. Y., Jia, Y., Hill, D. J., Xu, Y., & Zhang, Y. (2017). Short-term residential load forecasting based on LSTM recurrent neural network. IEEE Transactions on Smart Grid, 10(1), 841–851.

Loyola, M. C. B., Bueno, J. B., & De Leon, R. D. (2019). Internet-based electric meter with theft detection, theft notification and consumption monitoring for residential power lines using wireless network technology. International Journal of Electrical and Electronic Engineering and Telecommunications, 8(6), 332–338.

Nebrida, A. P., Amador, C. D., Madiam, C. M., & Ranche, G. J. S. (2023). Development of smart meter to monitor real time energy consumption for sustainability. International Journal of Sustainable Construction Engineering and Technology, 14(4), 21–30.

Onile, A. E., Belikov, J., Petlenkov, E., & Levron, Y. (2023). Leveraging digital twins and demand side recommender chatbot for optimizing smart grid energy efficiency. In Proceedings of the 2023 IEEE PES Innovative Smart Grid Technologies – Asia (ISGT Asia) (pp. 1–5). IEEE.

Papaioannou, C., Tzitzios, I., Papaioannou, A., Dimara, A., Anagnostopoulos, C. N., & Krinidis, S. (2025). EnergiQ: A prescriptive large language model-driven intelligent platform for interpreting appliance energy consumption patterns. Sensors, 25(16), 4911\.

Republic Act No. 10173\. (2012). Data Privacy Act of 2012\. Republic of the Philippines.

Santos, A. G. (2020). Forecasting residential electricity demand in the Philippines using an error correction model. The Philippine Review of Economics, 57(1), 121–151.

Saout, T., Lardeux, F., & Saubion, F. (2024). An overview of data extraction from invoices. IEEE Access, 12, 19872–19886.

Sibiya, C. A., Ogudo, K. A., & Aladesanmi, E. J. (2024). Machine learning-based anomaly detection in residential electricity usage patterns using meter data — Case study (Msunduzi Municipality). In Proceedings of the 2024 IEEE PES/IAS PowerAfrica (pp. 1–5). IEEE.

United Nations General Assembly. (2015). Transforming our world: The 2030 agenda for sustainable development (Resolution A/RES/70/1).
